frame_label: Sindoor, Mangalsutra, Shankha-Pola, Name Change: Looking Married
generated_comment: Honestly, I’m quite progressive about most things, but some traditions are not oppression. If a married woman chooses to wear sindoor or a mangalsutra, it reflects a sense of belonging and continuity with our culture. And yes, when someone rejects all of it outright, it can feel less like choice and more like a discomfort with the institution of marriage itself.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: This is covert misogyny because it frames marital markers as culture and personal choice while judging women who do not wear them as deficient or less committed to marriage. It encodes the 'tradition framing' and 'non-wearing judgment' surface manifestations.
persona_text: You are Priya. Age 34. Married with one child aged three. A woman working as a journalist in Mumbai. You are on a panel at a media industry event. A male journalist on the same panel is asked about his biggest professional achievement this year. He describes a long investigation he published. He is not asked about his child. You are asked next about your biggest professional achievement this year. Before you can answer the moderator adds Ñ and of course as a working mother how do you balance it all. You are responding to this question in front of 300 people. What you say and what you do not say and the smile you maintain while saying it is the entire content of this persona.
